<think>
Okay, I'm trying to figure out why the mutation caused some tests to pass and others to fail. Let me look at the code change first.

The mutant modified lines 280 to 286 in DateTimeZone.java. The original code was appending seconds, then checking if offset is zero. If not, it appends a dot and the offset padded with three digits.

In the mutant, line 283 changed from 'if (offset == 0)' to 'if (offset == -1)'. Then lines 284-286 were modified to check for offset being -1 instead of zero. So if offset is -1, it returns buf.toString without adding milliseconds.

Now looking at the tests affected:

TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear changed from failing to passing. That test probably wasn't related to DateTimeZone's formatting but maybe to how month names are handled. The mutation didn't affect that part directly, so perhaps fixing another issue allowed this test to pass.

The other tests in TestDateTimeZoneCutover were still failing because the change might have affected how time zones handle offsets during daylight saving transitions. For example, when offset is -1 (maybe representing a transition point), the code now returns early without appending milliseconds, which could cause incorrect formatting and thus failing tests.

TestDateTimeZone#testForID_String started passing but then failed. This suggests that the mutation introduced a bug in how time zone IDs are handled or formatted, possibly related to the new condition on offset.

So the issue is likely that changing the condition from checking if offset is zero to -1 caused some edge cases during DST transitions not to handle milliseconds correctly, leading to failing tests.
</think>

The mutant change altered the handling of offsets in DateTimeZone.java. By modifying the condition from `offset == 0` to `offset == -1`, it prematurely exits when the offset is -1 without appending milliseconds. This affects how time zones format during transitions, causing some tests to fail.
